title: I Want to Die but I Want to Eat Tteokbokki

chapters.title: Hard days leave little to say
chapters.text:
When was the last time a real moment of happiness showed up? The honest answer for most people is that the date is hard to pin down. Plenty of nights end with staring at the ceiling, replaying every word and every choice of the day, and that pattern is far from rare.

Nobody hands out a manual for being an adult. The lessons come through living.

Baek Sehee knows that low feeling from the inside. She grew up with low self-worth and emotional harm, and those years folded her adult life into something fragile. She chose to share her story so others could find the words for their own pain and a small light at the end of the long stretch.

When life feels heavy, the feeling is real and valid. It is not selfish to feel it most. As Sehee has written, a hard day is allowed to feel like the hardest day.

chapters.title: Save some kindness for yourself
chapters.text:
Sehee's story starts with a small red button on a recorder in a therapist's office. The recorder belongs to her, not the doctor. The reason matters, and by the end, a listener may find themselves surprised at what they learn from those tapes. For now, the key points are simple.

It may really be depression. The perfect image people chase is never real. There is no need to be a liar to oneself. There is no rule that says a person has to love everything.

Sehee's childhood was painful in ways that should not have come as a surprise. Her father was harsh in word and action. Her sister gave love only on her own terms. At school, her eczema marked her as different. Later, money and a dream job did not heal the pain or lift her sense of worth. Instead, she grew tangled in other people and afraid of her own real self.

The list below covers habits that often go with low self-worth. See which ones sound familiar.

Staying quiet in relationships. Keeping people at arm's length. Worrying about bothering others, about being unfair, or about being responsible for fixing other people's behavior. Pushing the self toward an ideal image. Pretending to love things the self does not actually like. Being hard on the self while turning away from support the body actually wants.

That is a long list. If several items felt close to home, nothing is broken. It may simply mean care is flowing outward while none is left for the self.

Kindness and care only help when they do not trap a person in the role of a martyr.

The right to feel angry, frustrated, or bored is real. Take movies as an example. If a film is not enjoyable, there is no need to rewatch it just to catch what others saw. The film simply did not click, and that is a valid view. And what about receiving care? After a night out, a friend calls a cab. The next morning, shame might creep in for no reason. Most people have been the one ordering the cab for a friend. That friend cared. The cab moment shows the care flows both ways, and that is worth sitting with.

The moment a person sets out to be more caring is the moment the task becomes a burden. As Sehee's therapist once said, empathy that is performed becomes a chore.

chapters.title: They may know that you know
chapters.text:
Humans are social, which sounds simple but has its traps. How often is a unique part of the self given up to keep a relationship steady, friendly or romantic? It gets called compromise, but sometimes the balance tips too far. The funny part is that other people sense it, and the reasons usually sit in a few places.

A feeling of being chosen and a pull to repay it. A slow boredom with comfort. A habit of swinging between extremes.

When a new and exciting person enters life, the natural reaction is to feel lucky and, in a quiet way, special. At the same time, a question can surface. Why did this person choose me? Without an honest talk, the mind starts to feel smaller and clings tighter, as if the attention must be paid back. The other person feels that grip and pulls away. The truth is that the fear of being left feeds the very behavior that drives them away. A few simple steps help.

Be honest and share the feeling. Most friends turn out to carry the same worries. Stop trying to predict the next move or measure every reaction. Stay in the moment and let the conversation breathe. Practice stepping back from extremes. Seeing every relationship in black and white terms is a fast way to burn out.

Without trying, the self copies the people it loves. They can feel what is felt, and that feeling runs both ways.

The idea may sound vague, so a story from Sehee helps. She made a friend she thought was more interesting than her. She opened up about the worry, and to her surprise, the friend felt the same way.

What came next was a quiet shock. Sehee realized her friend was not really above her. The attention she had been chasing began to wear her down. She had been seeing her friend only in best and worst moments, not as a real person.

Judging too quickly is unfair. It is normal to enjoy attention and feel worn out by it at the same time. What is not healthy is laying an ideal image on top of another person, because that image deepens the swings and turns them sour.

chapters.title: Pretty, not ugly
chapters.text:
Black and white thinking can color how a person sees their own looks, just as it colors behavior. A person can try to manage their image the same way they manage their actions. Picture a small case. You suggest a book, and a friend does not enjoy it. Instead of seeing it as a difference in taste, the mind reads it as a personal blow. The sting is real, and a few reasons tend to sit behind it.

A harsh inner judge. A suppressed sense of self. A drive to please everyone. A fear of losing the spotlight.

When the standard held for the self is impossibly high, every small flaw can feel like a reason to walk away. But a person does not throw away a friend for tiny human slip-ups.

The book example makes it clear. A friend says the story did not work for them, and the mind turns it into a personal failure. Their dislike suddenly exposes bad taste, and a flash of anger at the friend follows. The real target sits inside.

Self-esteem is a delicate tool. Turned up too high, it can turn the self into a harsh judge of the way it breathes, because the ideal version of the self supposedly breathes differently. That sterile model does not exist. It is made of nothing the person has actually lived, thought, or built. A person has their own opinions because their mind grew from their own life, just like a friend grew from theirs. Different opinions do not mean the friend is trying to tear the self down.

A small drop in self-expectations can make the self far easier to live with.

The same logic touches appearance. Many people want attention, and a few panic when the spotlight leaves them. That panic is not vanity. It comes from the same extreme thinking. For them, no attention feels like hate. They struggle to accept that they look fine on most days, and a plain you look normal feels like a wound. If that rings true, try breaking down the face of a favorite celebrity. Most of them look quite ordinary up close.

Some people with histrionic personality disorder carry a low self-image and a strong need to be noticed.

chapters.title: That tricky sense of self
chapters.text:
Self-esteem is a moving target, and setbacks are part of the path. Having a setback means there is a recent better moment to compare it to, and that is real progress. The next part looks at the moments when failure shows up.

The areas that scare people the most often sit in a few groups.

Love and friendship. Work and career. Dreams and plans.

Sehee had her own version of that list. Her mixed feelings about a new friend slowly shifted from thoughts like, she must think I have no life if she dumps her problems on me, to kinder ones like, she must find me comforting, I guess I am a good friend after all. Even with that shift, the inner battle left her embarrassed, especially when she opened up to her friend.

Honesty is hard because it lets other people step inside the soul.

Can a person stop thoughts they do not want? It can feel pointless. Even harder is taking a compliment. Trusting the sincerity of another person is a quiet sign of healthy self-worth. When self-worth is low, a compliment sounds like a hidden motive. The sad twist is that people with very high self-worth often struggle to take it down a notch. They reject useful feedback because it brushes a sore spot. A person has to know their own value first, or any outside credit will slide off.

How does unhealthy self-worth show up at work? The most common example is the imposter feeling. Sehee picked apart her own work after it was handed to a different team. The negative flow in her mind did not stop until it filled the room. She also felt humiliated by the move, as if a status had been taken from her.

If that feeling is familiar, remember that it is a view, not a fact. Pulling apart skills and experience to prove uselessness does not help. Try listing strengths in the third person if writing them in the first feels awkward. The same idea covers dreams and plans. A person may not notice burnout while doing what they love. The feeling of being unworthy often comes from mental and physical tiredness. Watch for it, and rest or take a break when the body asks for it.

chapters.title: Put yourself first
chapters.text:
Love is a tangled thing, and that is plain. Whether the love is for the self or for family, friends, or a partner, effort is the common thread. When a person is too tired or too drained to give the love they want, they end up torturing themselves, and the relationships do not look the way they hoped. Because love is tangled, it helps to break it into simple parts.

Forgiveness. Acceptance. A turn around.

Forgiveness takes a relationship out of stalemate. The mud of mutual blame hurts the most between people who share blood. In a perfect world, parents are the people known best, so their stings cut twice as deep. The residue of their criticism often comes from dreams they never chased, and it sticks until a person decides to peel it off, often with help. Sehee's mother was no different. She called herself stupid and insecure, and passed that infection to her daughters while blaming them for being weak enough to catch it. Forgiving layered harm takes time and effort, but it is also a form of love. By forgiving those closest, the soil is cleared for new growth.

What if the person doing the hurting is the self? The rules do not change. The answer still sits inside. Is there a part of the self that needs healing? Sehee remembers her aunt, who stepped away from the family after years of caring for an aging mother and young nieces. As a child, Sehee was angry at her. Later, she saw that her aunt had been alone, with no one to lean on.

The understanding comes with age, whether the self likes it or not.

After forgiveness comes acceptance. Sehee's aunt is a clear example. A person often sees a parent's pain more clearly once they realize that parent had to carry a child at the same age. Many young people stay child-free not only because of money, but also because of the emotional load. They spend their younger years on self-growth and healing, sometimes with a therapist, just as Sehee did.

The recorder clicks off. In the early sessions, Sehee recorded every meeting to study what she had said. She still wrestles with herself, but the healing comes in waves, moving back and forth. The faster the pattern is seen, the sooner a person can stand where Sehee stands.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Baek Sehee's story crosses borders and cultures. Most people share similar experiences and wounds, and those wounds travel through countries and generations. That shared weight does not make anyone wrong. Her story points to a simple truth. In our humanity, we are equal. We carry odd thoughts, sink into low moods, and lose our sense of meaning, sometimes all in one day. We are not gentle with ourselves, so why would the world be.

The lesson is to learn how to care for the self. Nobody else will do it, partly because other people are not caring for themselves either. Breaking the cycle of self-sabotage and neglect is worth trying.

A small practice helps. Write down the things that cause distress, and find a steady therapist to work through them. For readers who want to stay with Sehee, her second book, I Want to Die but I Still Want to Eat Tteokbokki, came out in June 2024.
